mnemonics: Growing & Shrinking (2PL Phases): Think of a plant. First it grows (acquires all locks), then it shrinks (releases all locks). You can't release a lock until you've finished growing.
keyPoints: Two-phase locking protocol consists of two phases: a growing phase, where a transaction can only acquire locks, and a shrinking phase, where a transaction can only release locks.
It guarantees conflict serializability, ensuring that the schedule of concurrent transactions is serializable.
Strict 2PL, a common variant, requires a transaction to hold all its exclusive (write) locks until it commits or aborts, which ensures recoverability and avoids cascading rollbacks.
commonMistakes: Releasing a lock during the growing phase, which violates the protocol's rule that locks can only be acquired in this phase.
Assuming 2PL guarantees freedom from deadlock; while it ensures serializability, it can still lead to deadlocks that must be handled by the system.
Confusing general 2PL with Strict 2PL and releasing write locks before a transaction commits, which can lead to cascading rollbacks.
